Customer Objective: Create accountable business ownership for AI in this customer environment.
Detailed Requirement: Name a customer-side executive sponsor who approves target populations, intended use cases, budget, and rollout decisions.
Cadence: At initial rollout and annual renewal
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: SharePoint list or decision log stored in Microsoft 365
Evidence of Completion: Signed charter or decision log naming sponsor and delegate.
Fail Condition: No named sponsor with authority to approve rollout scope.
Why it Matters: AI programs drift quickly when no one can resolve tradeoffs between speed, risk, and budget.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents AI from becoming an orphaned initiative by giving leadership clear ownership, decision rights, and direction.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns AI decisions when priorities, risk, and budget collide?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CEO/COO | Executive Sponsor: CIO

Customer Objective: Tie AI to specific work outcomes rather than vague productivity claims.
Detailed Requirement: Define one to three outcomes for each pilot persona with baseline metrics and success targets.
Cadence: Before pilot launch and before each department expansion
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Excel, Lists, or Power BI for baseline and target register
Evidence of Completion: Outcome matrix with baseline, target, owner, and review date.
Fail Condition: Pilot begins without clear outcome targets by persona.
Why it Matters: This prevents "turn it on and hope" deployment.
End Customer Business Value: Helps the customer tie AI to measurable outcomes so budget owners can see what is working and what should scale.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you proving AI value by team today?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CFO/CEO | Business Owner: COO

Customer Objective: Constrain rollout to a deliberate population inside the customer environment.
Detailed Requirement: Define the initial pilot group, their job functions, use cases, support path, and exit criteria for scale.
Evidence of Completion: Pilot roster, group export, and stage-gate criteria.
Fail Condition: Users are enabled broadly without a defined cohort and stage gate.
Why it Matters: Containment is one of the best early safeguards.
End Customer Business Value: Improves rollout discipline so the customer can start smaller, learn faster, and scale with confidence.
Customer Conversation Track: What is your plan to pilot cleanly and scale without chaos?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Executive Team | Department Leaders | IT Lead

Customer Objective: Set user expectations before AI is introduced.
Detailed Requirement: Publish a customer-facing communication covering what is enabled, what is not, where to get help, and what success looks like.
Evidence of Completion: Published launch notice and FAQ path.
Fail Condition: Users are enabled without written expectations or support guidance.
Why it Matters: Adoption quality depends on expectation setting.
End Customer Business Value: Improves rollout discipline so the customer can start smaller, learn faster, and scale with confidence.
Customer Conversation Track: What is your plan to pilot cleanly and scale without chaos?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Executive Team | Department Leaders | IT Lead

Customer Objective: Keep business, IT, and security aligned after launch.
Detailed Requirement: Run a recurring AI steering forum to review adoption, risk, roadmap changes, and unresolved exceptions.
Cadence: Monthly or quarterly depending on customer size
Evidence of Completion: Recurring meeting, agenda, and decision log.
Fail Condition: No recurring customer governance forum exists after launch.
Why it Matters: This is the customer-side governance heartbeat.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents AI from becoming an orphaned initiative by giving leadership clear ownership, decision rights, and direction.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns AI decisions when priorities, risk, and budget collide?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CEO/COO | Executive Sponsor: CIO

Customer Objective: Sequence AI work by value and risk.
Detailed Requirement: Maintain a backlog of proposed use cases with owner, business value, sensitivity, dependency, and readiness status.
Evidence of Completion: Backlog with prioritization criteria and current owner.
Fail Condition: New use cases are introduced ad hoc with no review against value or risk.
Why it Matters: This keeps growth deliberate rather than random.
End Customer Business Value: Improves rollout discipline so the customer can start smaller, learn faster, and scale with confidence.
Customer Conversation Track: What is your plan to pilot cleanly and scale without chaos?

Customer Objective: Avoid uncontrolled spend as adoption expands.
Detailed Requirement: Define which user populations receive paid AI capability, who stays limited, and which spend thresholds trigger review.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Microsoft 365 admin center usage and license reports
Evidence of Completion: License allocation plan and quarterly review note.
Fail Condition: Licenses expand without budget owner or review threshold.
Why it Matters: AI shelfware and surprise spend erode trust quickly.
End Customer Business Value: Keeps AI spend aligned to adoption and business value, reducing waste and surprise renewals.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you controlling AI spend before it turns into shelfware?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CFO/CEO | Business Owner: COO

Customer Objective: Require evidence before moving beyond the pilot.
Detailed Requirement: Document the minimum adoption, support, training, KPI, and data-readiness conditions required before each expansion wave.
Evidence of Completion: Signed expansion gate checklist for each new department.
Fail Condition: Broader rollout occurs before readiness gates are checked.
Why it Matters: Scaling too fast is one of the main failure modes.
End Customer Business Value: Improves rollout discipline so the customer can start smaller, learn faster, and scale with confidence.
Customer Conversation Track: What is your plan to pilot cleanly and scale without chaos?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Executive Team | Department Leaders | IT Lead

Customer Objective: Give the customer a sequenced view of AI maturity and dependencies.
Detailed Requirement: Maintain a roadmap across pilots, hardening, workflow automation, sensitive workloads, and agentic use cases.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Project for the web / Planner / Power BI
Evidence of Completion: Roadmap with owners, dependencies, and target quarter.
Fail Condition: Major AI initiatives exist with no forward plan or dependency mapping.
Why it Matters: A roadmap helps prevent fragmented point deployments.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents AI from becoming an orphaned initiative by giving leadership clear ownership, decision rights, and direction.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns AI decisions when priorities, risk, and budget collide?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CEO/COO | Executive Sponsor: CIO

Customer Objective: Clarify what the customer will and will not automate.
Detailed Requirement: Document acceptable autonomy, external AI usage, regulated data boundaries, and customer-facing automation limits.
Cadence: Annual and after major policy change
Evidence of Completion: Approved risk appetite statement with revision history.
Fail Condition: Sensitive AI decisions are made without a clear policy position on risk tolerance.
Why it Matters: This becomes crucial before deeper automation or agentic rollout.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents AI from becoming an orphaned initiative by giving leadership clear ownership, decision rights, and direction.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns AI decisions when priorities, risk, and budget collide?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CEO/COO | Executive Sponsor: CIO

Customer Objective: Set minimum day-to-day rules for customer employees.
Detailed Requirement: Publish an AI acceptable use policy covering approved tools, prohibited data, human review expectations, and how to report harmful output.
Evidence of Completion: Versioned policy and acknowledgement record.
Fail Condition: AI is enabled without a published policy acknowledged by in-scope users.
Why it Matters: Policy is the minimum social contract for safe usage.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces preventable employee mistakes by making safe AI usage clear, practical, and enforceable.
Customer Conversation Track: Do employees know what they can use AI for and what is off-limits?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO/Legal | Policy Owner: Compliance

Customer Objective: Give employees a clean list of what is allowed.
Detailed Requirement: Maintain an approved tools inventory covering copilots, assistants, browser tools, embedded AI, automations, and agent platforms.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: SharePoint list; Defender visibility where available
Evidence of Completion: Approved tools register with owner, purpose, and approval date.
Fail Condition: Employees are expected to use AI safely without an approved list of options.
Why it Matters: Start with what is allowed, not just what is blocked.
End Customer Business Value: Shrinks shadow AI risk by giving the customer a safer path than uncontrolled tool sprawl.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you handling shadow AI and unvetted tools already in use?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO | Technical Owner: CIO

Customer Objective: Reduce accidental exposure of sensitive content in AI tools.
Detailed Requirement: Document which classes of data may not be pasted, uploaded, summarized, or acted on in AI tools without exception.
Cadence: Annual review and upon regulatory change
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Purview classification references; SharePoint policy page
Evidence of Completion: Published prohibited-data list with real examples.
Fail Condition: Users cannot clearly tell what data is out-of-bounds for AI.
Why it Matters: Employees need concrete examples, not only abstract labels.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces preventable employee mistakes by making safe AI usage clear, practical, and enforceable.
Customer Conversation Track: Do employees know what they can use AI for and what is off-limits?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO/Legal | Policy Owner: Compliance

Customer Objective: Require users to verify important AI output.
Detailed Requirement: Define when employees must fact-check, cite, peer review, manager review, or refuse to use AI-generated content.
Evidence of Completion: Published validation standard with role-based examples.
Fail Condition: Customer-facing or sensitive output may be used with no validation requirement.
Why it Matters: Human review is still the core control for unreliable output.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces preventable employee mistakes by making safe AI usage clear, practical, and enforceable.
Customer Conversation Track: Do employees know what they can use AI for and what is off-limits?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO/Legal | Policy Owner: Compliance

Customer Objective: Prevent uncontrolled enablement of new AI capability in production.
Detailed Requirement: Require formal approval before turning on new AI features, browser extensions, connectors, or agents in the customer environment.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Microsoft 365 admin evidence stored with change record
Evidence of Completion: Approved change record with rollback notes and approver.
Fail Condition: Production AI features are turned on without change review and rollback path.
Why it Matters: New AI features often change the data boundary or behavior boundary.
End Customer Business Value: Creates a repeatable approval process so new AI capabilities can be adopted without bypassing governance.
Customer Conversation Track: What approval path exists before new AI tools or agents go live?

Customer Objective: Control data-handling risk for non-native AI services.
Detailed Requirement: Perform security, privacy, retention, legal, and supportability review before approving third-party AI services or plugins.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Defender for Cloud Apps references; SharePoint checklist
Evidence of Completion: Completed due diligence checklist and approval decision.
Fail Condition: Non-native AI tools are approved without risk review.
Why it Matters: Many real-world AI deployments sprawl through browser and SaaS tools first.
End Customer Business Value: Shrinks shadow AI risk by giving the customer a safer path than uncontrolled tool sprawl.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you handling shadow AI and unvetted tools already in use?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO | Technical Owner: CIO

Customer Objective: Keep exceptions rare, documented, and time bound.
Detailed Requirement: Track policy exceptions with owner, business justification, compensating controls, approver, and expiry date.
Evidence of Completion: Active exception register with no expired unreviewed entries.
Fail Condition: Exceptions are granted informally or do not expire.
Why it Matters: Unmanaged exceptions become the de facto policy.
End Customer Business Value: Creates a repeatable approval process so new AI capabilities can be adopted without bypassing governance.
Customer Conversation Track: What approval path exists before new AI tools or agents go live?

Customer Objective: Treat custom agents as governed production assets.
Detailed Requirement: Require a documented approval path before a custom agent or automation is published to employees or connected to production data.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Copilot Studio admin controls; Power Platform admin center
Evidence of Completion: Approval record, owner, data scope, and support tier.
Fail Condition: Custom agents are published without owner, approval, or scope review.
Why it Matters: Agents are software, not experiments, once they are published.
End Customer Business Value: Creates a repeatable approval process so new AI capabilities can be adopted without bypassing governance.
Customer Conversation Track: What approval path exists before new AI tools or agents go live?

Customer Objective: Align the customer contracts and notices with AI reality where needed.
Detailed Requirement: Review whether employee notices, customer commitments, privacy notices, or supplier terms need to be updated for AI use.
Cadence: Annual review and before customer-facing AI launch
Evidence of Completion: Legal review memo or clause update log.
Fail Condition: Customer-facing AI use expands with no legal review where notices or commitments are implicated.
Why it Matters: This matters more than many teams realize, especially for external AI or customer communications.
End Customer Business Value: Improves legal and audit defensibility as AI usage expands into more sensitive workflows and external interactions.
Customer Conversation Track: Are your policies and contracts keeping up with how AI is actually being used?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO/Legal | Policy Owner: Compliance

Customer Objective: Enforce policy where the risk profile requires technical restriction.
Detailed Requirement: Use technical controls to restrict unapproved public GenAI sites, desktop apps, and browser extensions unless explicitly approved.
Cadence: Quarterly review and upon major new AI service adoption
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint / Defender for Cloud Apps
Generic Tooling Category: DNS filtering / secure web gateway / CASB / browser control
Evidence of Completion: Validated block list, exception path, and successful control test.
Fail Condition: High-risk users can access unapproved AI tools with no compensating control.
Why it Matters: Policy-only governance is not enough for high-risk environments.
End Customer Business Value: Shrinks shadow AI risk by giving the customer a safer path than uncontrolled tool sprawl.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you handling shadow AI and unvetted tools already in use?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO | Technical Owner: CIO

Safeguard Title: Policy mapping to enterprise risk framework
Customer Objective: Align AI safeguards with broader risk and compliance obligations.
Detailed Requirement: Map AI safeguards to the customer security, privacy, legal, and audit framework and track gaps.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: SharePoint controlled register; Purview evidence exports where relevant
Evidence of Completion: Control mapping matrix and remediation actions.
Fail Condition: Regulated AI use proceeds without mapping to existing control obligations.
Why it Matters: This gives the framework defensibility in regulated or audit-heavy contexts.
End Customer Business Value: Improves legal and audit defensibility as AI usage expands into more sensitive workflows and external interactions.
Customer Conversation Track: Are your policies and contracts keeping up with how AI is actually being used?

Customer Objective: Test whether governance controls are actually shaping behavior.
Detailed Requirement: Review policy breaches, exception volume, training issues, and shadow AI trends to decide whether governance content needs revision.
Evidence of Completion: Review memo with revisions or accepted residual risk.
Fail Condition: Policy documents exist but are never reviewed against real user behavior.
Why it Matters: Governance must adapt to usage reality.
End Customer Business Value: Improves legal and audit defensibility as AI usage expands into more sensitive workflows and external interactions.
Customer Conversation Track: Are your policies and contracts keeping up with how AI is actually being used?

Customer Objective: Reduce account compromise risk before AI access is granted.
Detailed Requirement: Confirm SSO, MFA, and basic identity hygiene for all in-scope users.
Cadence: Before initial rollout and for each new user cohort
Evidence of Completion: Identity readiness checklist and policy evidence.
Fail Condition: AI access is granted to users without MFA or equivalent identity controls.
Why it Matters: AI often increases the blast radius of compromised accounts.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the chance that weak identity controls turn AI access into a larger security problem.
Customer Conversation Track: Is your identity stack ready so AI does not amplify account risk?

Customer Objective: Ensure employees use supported devices, browsers, and apps for targeted AI tools.
Detailed Requirement: Validate supported OS, browser, Office apps, extension posture, and update channels for the in-scope AI experience.
Evidence of Completion: Readiness report or compliance export.
Fail Condition: Pilot starts on unsupported devices or app versions.
Why it Matters: Bad endpoint state creates reliability and support problems fast.
End Customer Business Value: Avoids failed rollouts and support headaches by confirming the environment can reliably support AI.
Customer Conversation Track: Can your current environment reliably support the AI experience you want?

Customer Objective: Prevent rollout failure from blocked or unstable service paths.
Detailed Requirement: Verify required service endpoints, proxies, browser policies, and API reachability for in-scope AI tools.
Cadence: Per rollout wave and after major network change
Evidence of Completion: Connectivity checklist and test results.
Fail Condition: Critical AI services or APIs are unreachable from target networks.
Why it Matters: This matters more in locked-down, VDI, or proxied environments.
End Customer Business Value: Avoids failed rollouts and support headaches by confirming the environment can reliably support AI.
Customer Conversation Track: Can your current environment reliably support the AI experience you want?

Customer Objective: Make sure access can be paused or reversed safely.
Detailed Requirement: Document how AI access, licenses, connectors, and user targeting will be rolled back if risk or instability emerges.
Evidence of Completion: Documented rollback procedure and owner.
Fail Condition: The customer cannot quickly limit or reverse rollout.
Why it Matters: Reversibility is a core readiness control.
End Customer Business Value: Gives the customer a safer path to deploy, test, and reverse AI changes without major disruption.
Customer Conversation Track: If AI rollout causes issues, how quickly can you contain or reverse it?
Who Cares Most (Customer): IT Operations Owner | Platform Owner | CIO

Customer Objective: Ensure the environment has named administrators for the relevant control plane.
Detailed Requirement: Identify who owns identity, endpoint, collaboration, data, security, and AI-specific configuration for the customer environment.
Evidence of Completion: Named admin ownership matrix.
Fail Condition: No one can clearly state who owns key AI-related settings.
Why it Matters: Control gaps often arise at responsibility boundaries.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the chance that weak identity controls turn AI access into a larger security problem.
Customer Conversation Track: Is your identity stack ready so AI does not amplify account risk?

Customer Objective: Make sure the customer can handle issues when pilot users start using AI.
Detailed Requirement: Define support contacts, severity model, ticket categorization, and escalation route for AI-related issues.
Evidence of Completion: Support runbook and service category.
Fail Condition: Users are enabled with no support category or escalation route.

Customer Objective: Restrict higher-trust AI use to compliant devices where risk warrants.
Detailed Requirement: Require compliant or managed devices for sensitive use cases or user groups.
Evidence of Completion: Policy evidence and test from non-compliant device.
Fail Condition: Sensitive AI access remains available from unmanaged or non-compliant devices.
Why it Matters: This is particularly important for executives, finance, legal, HR, and high-value data access.

Customer Objective: Reduce blast radius from admin compromise or misconfiguration.
Detailed Requirement: Review and minimize standing admin access for AI configuration, connector management, and platform settings.
Evidence of Completion: Admin review export and remediation actions.
Fail Condition: Broad admin standing access persists for AI configuration surfaces.
Why it Matters: This is a standard security hygiene control adapted for AI admin surfaces.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the chance that weak identity controls turn AI access into a larger security problem.

Customer Objective: Know what happened when something goes wrong.
Detailed Requirement: Confirm audit logging is enabled for AI activity, admin changes, data access, and connector activity.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Purview Audit; Entra logs; Microsoft 365 audit
Evidence of Completion: Audit log coverage confirmation and retention setting.
Fail Condition: AI activity occurs without audit coverage for security or compliance investigation.
Why it Matters: Logging is foundational for incident response, compliance, and forensics.
End Customer Business Value: Preserves the evidence needed to investigate incidents, support compliance reviews, and resolve disputes faster.
Customer Conversation Track: If something goes wrong, do you have the logs to prove what happened?

Customer Objective: Prevent experimental AI from affecting production users or data.
Detailed Requirement: Maintain separate environments for development, testing, and production for custom agents, automations, and integrations.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Copilot Studio / Power Platform environments / Entra groups
Evidence of Completion: Environment inventory and promotion evidence.
Fail Condition: Custom AI is tested in production with production data and production users.
Why it Matters: This is basic SDLC discipline applied to AI assets.
End Customer Business Value: Gives the customer a safer path to deploy, test, and reverse AI changes without major disruption.
Customer Conversation Track: If AI rollout causes issues, how quickly can you contain or reverse it?

Safeguard Title: Backup / recovery considerations for AI-managed workflows
Customer Objective: Ensure AI-managed processes can be recovered if disrupted.
Detailed Requirement: Review whether workflows, data sources, or configurations managed by AI have adequate backup, versioning, or restore capability.
Cadence: Annual review and before major automation rollout
Evidence of Completion: Review memo or checklist covering key AI-managed workflows.
Fail Condition: AI-managed workflows have no considered recovery path.
Why it Matters: This is an emerging concern as AI takes over more operational processes.
End Customer Business Value: Gives the customer a safer path to deploy, test, and reverse AI changes without major disruption.
Customer Conversation Track: If AI rollout causes issues, how quickly can you contain or reverse it?
Who Cares Most (Customer): IT Operations Owner | Platform Owner | CIO

Customer Objective: Track what AI connects to inside the customer environment.
Detailed Requirement: Maintain an inventory of connectors, APIs, data sources, and external services used by AI tools and agents.
Evidence of Completion: Dependency register with owner and review date.
Fail Condition: AI integrations exist without a tracked dependency or owner.
Why it Matters: Dependencies become risk when they change or fail without notice.
End Customer Business Value: Protects the AI control plane by clarifying ownership and reducing risk in integrations, credentials, and sensitive workloads.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns the platform, integrations, and secrets behind your AI stack?

Customer Objective: Eliminate standing admin access for sensitive AI configuration.
Detailed Requirement: Require PIM or equivalent just-in-time elevation for privileged AI configuration changes.
Evidence of Completion: PIM activation logs and role assignment evidence.
Fail Condition: Admins retain standing privileged access to AI configuration surfaces.
Why it Matters: JIT is best practice for sensitive admin surfaces.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the chance that weak identity controls turn AI access into a larger security problem.
Customer Conversation Track: Is your identity stack ready so AI does not amplify account risk?

Customer Objective: Protect API keys, tokens, and credentials used by AI integrations.
Detailed Requirement: Store and rotate secrets used by AI connectors and agents in a managed vault with access logging.
Evidence of Completion: Vault configuration and access log evidence.
Fail Condition: AI integration credentials are stored in code, config files, or unmanaged locations.
Why it Matters: Credential compromise is a common attack vector for AI integrations.
End Customer Business Value: Protects the AI control plane by clarifying ownership and reducing risk in integrations, credentials, and sensitive workloads.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns the platform, integrations, and secrets behind your AI stack?

Customer Objective: Contain AI risk for sensitive user populations or data.
Detailed Requirement: Design and document isolation patterns for high-risk users, sensitive data, or regulated workloads that interact with AI.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Entra groups; Conditional Access; SharePoint access segmentation
Evidence of Completion: Isolation design document and implementation evidence.
Fail Condition: High-risk AI use cases share the same access and data boundaries as general use.
Why it Matters: Isolation reduces blast radius for the most sensitive scenarios.
End Customer Business Value: Protects the AI control plane by clarifying ownership and reducing risk in integrations, credentials, and sensitive workloads.
Customer Conversation Track: Who owns the platform, integrations, and secrets behind your AI stack?

Customer Objective: Constrain Copilot rollout to a named pilot cohort with deliberate access boundaries.
Detailed Requirement: Use group-based license assignment and Integrated Apps scoping so Microsoft 365 Copilot is assigned only to a named pilot group. Align the tenant app scope to the same group and document the initial cohort size, owners, and access path.
Cadence: Before first pilot launch and per rollout wave
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Entra ID group-based licensing; M365 Admin Integrated Apps
Evidence of Completion: Named pilot group, license assignment evidence, and app scoping screenshot or export.
Fail Condition: Copilot licenses or app access are assigned broadly with no pilot group or scoped app boundary.
Why it Matters: Phased access is one of the most important early Copilot controls because it limits blast radius while the environment, data, and support model are still being proven.
End Customer Business Value: Improves rollout discipline and reduces avoidable support noise by controlling exactly who gets Copilot first.
Customer Conversation Track: Who gets Copilot first, and how are you preventing accidental broad rollout?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CIO/COO | Technical Owner: Entra Admin

Safeguard Title: Self-service Copilot purchase and trial controls
Customer Objective: Prevent unmanaged Copilot spend and access from self-service purchase paths.
Detailed Requirement: Set Copilot self-service purchases to Do not allow and disable self-service trial options for Copilot. Document the approval path for any exception and confirm the tenant is not allowing unmanaged purchases.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: M365 Admin Copilot Control; License Controls
Evidence of Completion: Tenant setting screenshots showing self-service purchase disabled and trial option disabled.
Fail Condition: Users can purchase or start Copilot trials without IT approval.
Why it Matters: Self-service licensing creates surprise spend, inconsistent rollout, and weaker governance because users can bypass the intended approval path.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents surprise spend, shadow licensing, and uneven rollout patterns that erode trust in the Copilot program.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you preventing Copilot spend from leaking in through self-buy and free trials?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Economic Buyer: CFO/CEO | Business Owner: CIO

Customer Objective: Define the tenant baseline for whether Copilot can use web grounding at launch.
Detailed Requirement: Configure the cloud policy for Allow web search in Copilot and document the approved baseline. If disabled at launch, record the rationale, scope, and criteria for later review; if enabled, document the business and data-handling rationale.
Evidence of Completion: Policy export or screenshot and decision memo showing the approved grounding mode.
Fail Condition: Web grounding is left at default with no explicit tenant decision or documented rationale.
Why it Matters: Web grounding changes the data boundary, answer behavior, and user expectation model, so it should be an intentional tenant decision rather than a default left untouched.
End Customer Business Value: Creates a deliberate Copilot grounding posture so the customer can balance usefulness, data boundary, and user expectations.
Customer Conversation Track: Should Copilot answer only from your environment at launch, or also use the public web?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Risk Buyer: CISO | Executive Sponsor: CIO

Customer Objective: Limit SharePoint grounding to reviewed sites before broad knowledge access begins.
Detailed Requirement: Enable Restricted SharePoint Search and maintain a minimal allowed-site list based on reviewed permissions. Record which sites are intentionally in scope for Copilot grounding and which remain excluded until access hygiene is validated.
Evidence of Completion: Restricted SharePoint Search enabled, approved site list, and review notes for included sites.
Fail Condition: Copilot can search broad SharePoint content with no approved allow-list or search scope decision.
Why it Matters: Copilot amplifies existing SharePoint permissions and search scope, so grounding should begin from validated sites rather than from the entire tenant by default.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces oversharing risk and improves answer quality by grounding Copilot only on validated SharePoint sources.
Customer Conversation Track: What content is Copilot allowed to ground on first, and how are you avoiding SharePoint oversharing?

Safeguard Title: Teams Copilot meeting and transcript baseline
Customer Objective: Set the launch policy for Copilot in Teams meetings, calls, and transcript-backed recaps.
Detailed Requirement: Define meeting policies for Copilot in Teams meetings and calls, including whether transcription is enabled, whether no-transcript Copilot is disabled, and what organizer/participant notice is required. Document the approved meeting policy set for the pilot cohort.
Evidence of Completion: Meeting policy export, transcript setting evidence, and pilot policy assignment.
Fail Condition: Meeting Copilot or transcription is enabled without an explicit policy decision for the pilot group.
Why it Matters: Meeting Copilot changes privacy expectations, recap quality, and meeting policy behavior, especially when transcript decisions have not been made deliberately.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents confusion, privacy surprises, and inconsistent recap behavior by treating meeting Copilot as a governed feature, not a default assumption.
Customer Conversation Track: What is your policy for Copilot in meetings and calls, and are transcripts part of that design?

Customer Objective: Set the baseline for who can access, install, and create Copilot agents.
Detailed Requirement: Configure who can access and install agents, restrict third-party agents from the store at baseline, and scope custom or org-built agents to approved groups or use cases. Record the approved baseline for agent access sources and user populations.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: M365 Admin Copilot Control; Copilot Studio / Agents
Evidence of Completion: Agent access settings export, blocked third-party store posture, and scoped access group list.
Fail Condition: Agents are broadly accessible, third-party store agents are allowed, or no approved source policy exists.
Why it Matters: Agent access expands the capability and data boundary of Copilot quickly, so a baseline source and audience model is required before agent usage spreads.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents uncontrolled agent sprawl and keeps early Copilot usage focused on approved experiences with known data boundaries.
Customer Conversation Track: Who can install or use agents today, and what is stopping third-party agents from showing up by default?

Safeguard Title: Managed device and browser Copilot containment
Customer Objective: Align managed-device and browser Copilot behavior to the enterprise rollout policy.
Detailed Requirement: Configure Edge and Windows controls for Copilot on managed devices, including whether the Copilot button is shown, whether page content access is allowed, and whether consumer Copilot app installation is blocked or removed. Document the managed-device baseline for Copilot access surfaces.
Evidence of Completion: Policy export for Edge page-content setting, Windows/Intune consumer app restriction, and device scope evidence.
Fail Condition: Managed devices allow consumer Copilot installation or broad page-content access with no approved baseline.
Why it Matters: Consumer Copilot surfaces and permissive browser context settings can undermine enterprise controls by creating unmanaged access paths on corporate devices.
End Customer Business Value: Protects the enterprise rollout from side-door consumer experiences and limits accidental exposure of browser content.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you keeping managed-device Copilot behavior aligned with your enterprise policy?

Safeguard Title: Copilot audit and usage telemetry baseline
Customer Objective: Verify that Copilot audit, telemetry, and usage visibility exist before broad adoption begins.
Detailed Requirement: Enable Unified Audit Log coverage for Copilot, verify Copilot interaction events are being captured, and confirm Copilot usage reports or dashboards are available to the right admin audience. Document who reviews the data and how often.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Purview Audit; Viva / Analytics; M365 Admin Center
Evidence of Completion: Audit event verification, usage report access evidence, and review cadence notes.
Fail Condition: Copilot is live without verified audit coverage or no one is assigned to review usage and telemetry.
Why it Matters: Without verified audit and usage visibility, support, compliance, and adoption conversations rely on assumptions instead of evidence.
End Customer Business Value: Gives the customer evidence for support, compliance, and ROI conversations instead of guesswork once Copilot is live.
Customer Conversation Track: If Copilot is live, what reports prove usage, and what logs prove what happened?

Customer Objective: Govern how Copilot Pages and Notebooks are created, stored, and shared as adoption expands.
Detailed Requirement: Define the approved baseline for Create and view Copilot Pages and Create and view Copilot Notebooks, including who can create them, where they are stored, and how sharing is reviewed. Align this to SharePoint sharing standards for the rollout cohort.
Evidence of Completion: Policy evidence for Pages/Notebooks, storage and sharing guidance, and review checklist.
Fail Condition: Users can create and share Copilot Pages or Notebooks broadly with no content-governance standard.
Why it Matters: Pages and Notebooks turn chat output into shareable content stored in Microsoft 365, so they need the same sharing and content-governance discipline as other collaboration assets.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents new Copilot content types from becoming an unmanaged sharing channel as adoption grows.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you governing Copilot Pages and Notebooks once users begin saving and sharing outputs?

Safeguard Title: Sensitive site exclusion and access review
Customer Objective: Exclude or harden sensitive SharePoint sites before expanding Copilot grounding.
Detailed Requirement: Use Restricted Content Discovery, Restricted Access Control, and on-demand Site Access Review for sensitive or high-risk sites. Document which sites are excluded from Copilot and which sites require formal access attestation before being added to the grounding scope.
Evidence of Completion: List of excluded sites, site-level controls enabled where needed, and access review evidence.
Fail Condition: Sensitive sites remain discoverable by Copilot with no exclusion or access-attestation workflow.
Why it Matters: Not every site is equally ready for Copilot grounding, and site-level controls are often the safest way to expand search while containing sensitive content.
End Customer Business Value: Lets the customer expand Copilot search without treating every site as equally ready or equally safe.
Customer Conversation Track: What is your process for keeping Copilot away from sensitive sites while still expanding useful knowledge access?

Safeguard Title: Per-user SharePoint Copilot service plan scoping
Customer Objective: Use per-user SharePoint Copilot service plans when rollout readiness differs by user population.
Detailed Requirement: Use the Microsoft 365 Copilot for SharePoint service plan to disable SharePoint and agent-related Copilot experiences for specific users or groups when the broader Copilot license remains assigned. Document the criteria for who is in or out of SharePoint-grounded Copilot.
Evidence of Completion: User or group-level service plan evidence and approved inclusion criteria.
Fail Condition: All licensed users receive SharePoint-grounded Copilot with no user-level service-plan decision.
Why it Matters: User-level service plan scoping allows the rollout to stay precise when different groups are ready for different Copilot grounding experiences.
End Customer Business Value: Supports a more precise rollout by separating general Copilot access from SharePoint-grounded experiences where readiness differs.
Customer Conversation Track: Do some users need Copilot without SharePoint grounding, and do you have a control for that?

Customer Objective: Intentionally enable Teams features that improve Copilot meeting quality for approved cohorts.
Detailed Requirement: For approved meeting cohorts, define whether live captions, custom dictionary, Teams calls Copilot, translated transcription, or intelligent speaker features are enabled. Document the approved feature bundle by meeting policy and any consent or room-device prerequisites.
Evidence of Completion: Meeting policy bundle, feature enablement evidence, and pilot feedback or quality review.
Fail Condition: Expanded meeting features are enabled ad hoc with no approved bundle or role-based policy model.
Why it Matters: Once meeting Copilot is approved, optional meeting features can improve transcript quality and recap usefulness, but only when they are bundled deliberately and supported.
End Customer Business Value: Improves the usefulness of Copilot recaps and transcripts while keeping meeting feature changes deliberate and supportable.
Customer Conversation Track: Once meeting Copilot is allowed, which supporting Teams features are you turning on to improve output quality?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Executive Sponsor | Teams Owner | Enablement Owner

Customer Objective: Restrict agent publishing and use a blocked-list or request flow before organization-wide visibility expands.
Detailed Requirement: Restrict who can publish agents to the organization, maintain a blocked agents list, and enable an access-request approval flow for restricted agents where appropriate. Record the approved publisher group and the review process for blocked or requested agents.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Copilot Studio / Agents; M365 Admin Copilot Control
Evidence of Completion: Approved publisher group, blocked agents list, and request approval workflow evidence.
Fail Condition: Any creator can publish agents broadly or no blocked-list / request flow exists for restricted agents.
Why it Matters: Publishing rights determine when an internal experiment becomes an organization-wide asset, so publishing and blocked-list controls are a key maturity gate for agents.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents internal agent sprawl from becoming organization-wide risk and creates a cleaner path from prototype to approved asset.
Customer Conversation Track: Who can publish an agent to the whole company, and what is your approval path for blocked or restricted agents?

Safeguard Title: Store agent and app approval model
Customer Objective: Create an approval model for store agents, Microsoft-built agents, and Copilot extensions.
Detailed Requirement: Review Microsoft-built agents for relevance, disable or block agents not approved for the tenant, and maintain an approval process for any store-based extension, plugin, or add-in that extends Copilot. Record owner, purpose, data scope, and approval status for each approved experience.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: M365 Admin Copilot Control; Integrated Apps
Evidence of Completion: Approved agent or extension register with owner, data scope, and current approval status.
Fail Condition: Store agents or Copilot extensions are available without individual review or approval records.
Why it Matters: Store-delivered Copilot capabilities change quickly and often touch business data, so each approved experience needs an owner, scope, and review trail.
End Customer Business Value: Turns the Copilot ecosystem into a governed catalog instead of a moving target of unreviewed capabilities.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you approving Microsoft-built agents, third-party agents, and Copilot extensions one by one?

Safeguard Title: Copilot analytics audience and privacy controls
Customer Objective: Use Copilot analytics with a privacy-preserving access and audience model.
Detailed Requirement: Control access to the Copilot Dashboard, keep the minimum group size threshold at an approved privacy-preserving level, and document whether organizational data uploads to Viva Insights are in scope. Define who receives analytics and for what governance purpose.
Evidence of Completion: Dashboard access list, minimum group size setting, and analytics use standard or charter.
Fail Condition: Copilot analytics are broadly visible or privacy thresholds are reduced without approved rationale.
Why it Matters: Copilot dashboards are useful for adoption governance, but access and privacy thresholds should be deliberate to avoid exposing more behavioral detail than needed.
End Customer Business Value: Balances adoption visibility with privacy discipline so the customer can govern behavior trends without overreaching into individual monitoring.
Customer Conversation Track: Who can see Copilot adoption data, and what privacy floor protects individuals in those insights?

Customer Objective: Coordinate deployment, pinning, and app visibility changes by rollout cohort.
Detailed Requirement: Define the approved rollout posture for app deployment and pinning, including Windows taskbar pinning, pinning Copilot Chat in Microsoft 365 apps, the M365 Copilot app deployment setting, and any tenant opt-out from automatic deployment if reintroduced. Document the user cohorts that receive each visibility change.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: M365 Admin Copilot Control; Apps Admin Center; Windows / Intune
Evidence of Completion: Deployment and pinning settings export plus approved rollout wave matrix.
Fail Condition: Copilot visibility changes are left to default behavior with no approved user-cohort rollout design.
Why it Matters: Client visibility changes influence usage behavior, support demand, and user expectation, so they should follow readiness rather than happen by default.
End Customer Business Value: Improves adoption quality by matching Copilot visibility to readiness, training, and support capacity.
Customer Conversation Track: When should Copilot become more visible in apps and on devices, and for which user cohorts?

Customer Objective: Treat frontier Copilot features as controlled experiments with explicit approval and exit criteria.
Detailed Requirement: Document the governance model for Copilot Frontier access across web apps, desktop/mobile, and agents. Require named pilot groups, exit criteria, and explicit approval before any frontier feature is enabled outside a limited innovation cohort.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: M365 Admin Copilot Control; M365 Insider program
Evidence of Completion: Named frontier pilot list, approval record, and criteria for expansion or rollback.
Fail Condition: Frontier features are enabled outside a named pilot with no approval, success criteria, or rollback path.
Why it Matters: Preview and frontier capabilities can change behavior, supportability, and risk quickly, so they should not be treated like stable production features.
End Customer Business Value: Lets the customer explore new Copilot capabilities without turning preview volatility into production instability.
Customer Conversation Track: How are you experimenting with frontier Copilot features without exposing the broader organization?

Safeguard Title: Copilot Tuning and external model governance
Customer Objective: Control who can tune Copilot agents and whether non-Microsoft base models are ever approved.
Detailed Requirement: Scope Copilot Tuning to an approved group, document the business cases allowed for tuning, and keep open-source or external base model tuning disabled unless a separate risk review approves it. Maintain approval records for any tuning project.
Evidence of Completion: Approved tuning group, tuning project register, and external model posture evidence.
Fail Condition: Tuning is broadly available or external base models are allowed with no approved review standard.
Why it Matters: Tuning increases customization power but also increases model, support, and governance complexity, especially when external or open models are considered.
End Customer Business Value: Enables advanced Copilot customization without opening the door to uncontrolled model risk or unsupported tuning work.
Customer Conversation Track: If you want tailored Copilot behavior, who can tune it and what models are they allowed to use?

Customer Objective: Govern metered Copilot and agent billing before usage-based charges become operational spend.
Detailed Requirement: If Copilot pay-as-you-go billing or other metered Copilot services are in scope, document the approved owner, budget threshold, reporting cadence, and allowed use cases. Keep the setting unconfigured or off until those controls exist.
Evidence of Completion: Billing owner assignment, approved use-case list, and monthly spend review artifact.
Fail Condition: Metered Copilot billing is enabled with no owner, budget threshold, or use-case approval.
Why it Matters: Usage-based Copilot billing introduces variable spend that can scale faster than governance if no owner or threshold exists.
End Customer Business Value: Prevents advanced Copilot usage from becoming invisible variable spend with no accountable owner.
Customer Conversation Track: If Copilot usage can become metered, who owns the budget and what spend guardrails are in place?

Safeguard Title: Cross-surface Copilot and AI provider review
Customer Objective: Review advanced Copilot surfaces and non-Microsoft AI provider options before approving broader use.
Detailed Requirement: Review and approve any expansion into alternative AI providers for other LLMs, Edge Copilot Mode, or other advanced Copilot surfaces that materially change grounding, page context, or provider behavior. Record the approved surface, provider, and user scope.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: M365 Admin Copilot Control; Edge Management
Evidence of Completion: Review memo listing approved providers or surfaces, scope, and rationale.
Fail Condition: Alternative AI providers or advanced Copilot surfaces are enabled with no explicit review or user scope definition.
Why it Matters: New Copilot surfaces and provider options can change grounding, page-context behavior, and support assumptions, so they should be evaluated as intentional expansions.
End Customer Business Value: Keeps the Copilot estate coherent as new experiences emerge, instead of letting scattered surface changes outpace policy.
Customer Conversation Track: Which advanced Copilot surfaces or non-Microsoft AI providers are you willing to approve, and for whom?

Safeguard Title: Advanced SharePoint governance for Copilot content hygiene
Customer Objective: Use advanced SharePoint lifecycle and oversharing controls when Copilot readiness depends on content hygiene at scale.
Detailed Requirement: For tenants with significant SharePoint debt, evaluate SharePoint Advanced Management, inactive site policy, oversharing reports, and related lifecycle controls as a formal Copilot readiness workstream. Track remediation actions that improve the quality and safety of Copilot grounding over time.
Evidence of Completion: Governance report, inactive site remediation plan, and tracked actions from oversharing or lifecycle reviews.
Fail Condition: Copilot grounding continues to expand while stale sites, oversharing debt, or lifecycle issues remain unmanaged.
Why it Matters: Long-term Copilot quality and safety depend on the health of the SharePoint estate, not just on point-in-time access decisions.
End Customer Business Value: Improves long-term Copilot quality and reduces search noise by pairing Copilot expansion with ongoing content-governance cleanup.
Customer Conversation Track: Is SharePoint content hygiene good enough for Copilot at scale, or do you need a deeper remediation motion?

Customer Objective: Identify where AI will be used in real work.
Detailed Requirement: Document the top workflows where AI will be applied, including owner, frequency, data sensitivity, and expected AI role.
Evidence of Completion: Workflow inventory with owner and AI role.
Fail Condition: AI is enabled without identifying where it will actually be used.
Why it Matters: You cannot govern what you have not mapped.
End Customer Business Value: Moves AI from novelty to repeatable execution by documenting the workflows, prompts, and steps users should follow.
Customer Conversation Track: Which workflows are actually worth enabling first, and how will users execute them?

Customer Objective: Ensure humans remain in the loop for important decisions.
Detailed Requirement: Define which AI-assisted workflows require human review before output is used, sent, published, or acted on.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: SharePoint SOPs; Teams approvals where used
Evidence of Completion: Published human review requirements by workflow.
Fail Condition: AI output is used in important workflows without a defined human review point.
Why it Matters: Human review is the primary control for AI output risk.
End Customer Business Value: Protects quality and accountability by making it clear where human review still matters most.
Customer Conversation Track: Where must people stay in the loop before AI output is trusted or acted on?

Customer Objective: Give users clear instructions for approved AI workflows.
Detailed Requirement: Create standard operating procedures for each approved AI use case covering inputs, steps, validation, and escalation.
Cadence: Per workflow at launch and quarterly thereafter
Evidence of Completion: Published SOPs for each approved workflow.
Fail Condition: Users are expected to use AI effectively without written guidance.
Why it Matters: SOPs turn policy into practice.
End Customer Business Value: Moves AI from novelty to repeatable execution by documenting the workflows, prompts, and steps users should follow.
Customer Conversation Track: Which workflows are actually worth enabling first, and how will users execute them?

Customer Objective: Help users start with good patterns rather than trial and error.
Detailed Requirement: Curate and publish prompt templates for common workflows with guidance on when and how to use them.
Evidence of Completion: Published prompt library with usage guidance.
Fail Condition: Users are expected to craft effective prompts without examples or guidance.
Why it Matters: Good prompts reduce risk and improve output quality.
End Customer Business Value: Moves AI from novelty to repeatable execution by documenting the workflows, prompts, and steps users should follow.
Customer Conversation Track: Which workflows are actually worth enabling first, and how will users execute them?

Customer Objective: Measure whether AI is actually improving work.
Detailed Requirement: Establish baseline metrics for key workflows before and after AI enablement.
Evidence of Completion: Baseline and post-enablement metrics for key workflows.
Fail Condition: AI is deployed without baseline metrics to measure improvement.
Why it Matters: Without baselines, you cannot prove value or detect regression.
End Customer Business Value: Lets the customer measure whether AI is actually improving throughput, quality, or user experience.
Customer Conversation Track: How will you know whether AI improved the workflow or created rework?

Customer Objective: Ensure AI-related issues have a clear resolution path.
Detailed Requirement: Define escalation contacts and severity model for AI-related workflow issues.
Evidence of Completion: Published escalation path by workflow.
Fail Condition: AI workflow issues have no defined escalation or resolution path.
Why it Matters: Escalation clarity prevents small issues from becoming large ones.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces operational disruption by defining how AI-enabled workflows are changed, escalated, and shut down when needed.
Customer Conversation Track: What happens operationally when an AI-enabled workflow breaks or drifts?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Operations Lead | IT Lead | Department Owner

Customer Objective: Respond effectively when AI causes harm or unexpected behavior.
Detailed Requirement: Create an incident response playbook covering AI-specific scenarios including harmful output, data exposure, and automation failure.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Purview audit references; Teams incident collaboration
Evidence of Completion: Playbook with scenarios, owners, and tabletop evidence.
Fail Condition: AI incidents are handled ad hoc without a defined response process.
Why it Matters: AI incidents are different from traditional IT incidents.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces operational disruption by defining how AI-enabled workflows are changed, escalated, and shut down when needed.
Customer Conversation Track: What happens operationally when an AI-enabled workflow breaks or drifts?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Operations Lead | IT Lead | Department Owner

Customer Objective: Prevent uncontrolled changes to AI-assisted workflows.
Detailed Requirement: Require documented approval before modifying AI-assisted workflows, prompts, or automation logic.
Evidence of Completion: Change records with approval and rollback notes.
Fail Condition: AI workflow changes happen without review or documentation.
Why it Matters: Uncontrolled changes are a common source of regression.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces operational disruption by defining how AI-enabled workflows are changed, escalated, and shut down when needed.
Customer Conversation Track: What happens operationally when an AI-enabled workflow breaks or drifts?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Operations Lead | IT Lead | Department Owner

Customer Objective: Clarify where AI can act independently and where it cannot.
Detailed Requirement: Document which workflows allow AI to act autonomously, which require human approval, and which prohibit AI action entirely.
Evidence of Completion: Autonomy matrix with owner and review date.
Fail Condition: AI autonomy boundaries are undefined or inconsistently applied.
Why it Matters: This becomes critical before agentic AI deployment.
End Customer Business Value: Protects quality and accountability by making it clear where human review still matters most.
Customer Conversation Track: Where must people stay in the loop before AI output is trusted or acted on?

Customer Objective: Track whether AI-assisted workflows maintain quality over time.
Detailed Requirement: Create a quality scorecard for key AI-assisted workflows measuring accuracy, completeness, and user satisfaction.
Evidence of Completion: Quality scorecard with trend data.
Fail Condition: AI workflow quality is not measured after initial deployment.
Why it Matters: Quality can degrade silently without active monitoring.
End Customer Business Value: Lets the customer measure whether AI is actually improving throughput, quality, or user experience.
Customer Conversation Track: How will you know whether AI improved the workflow or created rework?

Customer Objective: Know when to stop or reverse an AI workflow.
Detailed Requirement: Document the conditions under which an AI-assisted workflow should be rolled back or retired.
Cadence: Annual review and after major issues
Evidence of Completion: Retirement criteria and decision authority.
Fail Condition: AI workflows persist indefinitely without review criteria.
Why it Matters: Not every AI workflow will succeed long-term.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces operational disruption by defining how AI-enabled workflows are changed, escalated, and shut down when needed.
Customer Conversation Track: What happens operationally when an AI-enabled workflow breaks or drifts?
Who Cares Most (Customer): Operations Lead | IT Lead | Department Owner

Customer Objective: Know what data AI can see and act on.
Detailed Requirement: Document which data repositories, document libraries, mailboxes, and sites are in scope for AI tools.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: SharePoint list; Purview data map references
Evidence of Completion: Data scope register with owner and classification.
Fail Condition: AI has access to data without a defined scope or owner.
Why it Matters: Data scope is the foundation of data security for AI.
End Customer Business Value: Defines clear AI data boundaries so the customer can unlock value without exposing the wrong content.
Customer Conversation Track: What data should AI see, and what should never be in scope?

Customer Objective: Label data so AI respects sensitivity boundaries.
Detailed Requirement: Define and apply a classification taxonomy that AI tools can reference for access and handling decisions.
Evidence of Completion: Published taxonomy and label application evidence.
Fail Condition: AI accesses data without classification or sensitivity context.
Why it Matters: Classification is the language AI uses to understand data sensitivity.
End Customer Business Value: Makes sensitive data usable with more confidence by pairing AI access with clear classification rules.
Customer Conversation Track: How does AI know what content is sensitive and how it must be handled?

Customer Objective: Reduce oversharing before AI amplifies it.
Detailed Requirement: Review and remediate overshared permissions in repositories that AI will access.
Evidence of Completion: Remediation report and before/after evidence.
Fail Condition: AI surfaces content through overshared permissions that users do not realize exist.
Why it Matters: Oversharing is the number one data risk in AI enablement.
End Customer Business Value: Finds and reduces hidden exposure that AI would otherwise surface faster and at greater scale.
Customer Conversation Track: Where is overshared content creating hidden AI exposure today?

Customer Objective: Clean up stale and redundant content before AI indexes it.
Detailed Requirement: Review repositories for stale, duplicate, or irrelevant content that could degrade AI quality or expose risk.
Evidence of Completion: Hygiene review report and remediation actions.
Fail Condition: AI indexes large volumes of stale or irrelevant content.
Why it Matters: Garbage in, garbage out applies directly to AI.
End Customer Business Value: Improves both defensibility and AI quality by keeping content sources clean, governed, and reviewable.
Customer Conversation Track: Is the content feeding AI clean, governed, and defensible when reviewed later?

Customer Objective: Ensure someone is responsible for each data scope.
Detailed Requirement: Assign named data owners for repositories and data classes that AI will access.
Evidence of Completion: Data ownership register with named owners.
Fail Condition: AI accesses data with no accountable owner.
Why it Matters: Ownership is the prerequisite for access decisions.
End Customer Business Value: Defines clear AI data boundaries so the customer can unlock value without exposing the wrong content.
Customer Conversation Track: What data should AI see, and what should never be in scope?

Customer Objective: Enforce classification through applied labels.
Detailed Requirement: Deploy sensitivity labels to in-scope repositories and require labeling for new content.
Evidence of Completion: Label deployment evidence and coverage report.
Fail Condition: Classification exists on paper but is not enforced through applied labels.
Why it Matters: Labels make classification actionable for AI and DLP.
End Customer Business Value: Makes sensitive data usable with more confidence by pairing AI access with clear classification rules.
Customer Conversation Track: How does AI know what content is sensitive and how it must be handled?

Customer Objective: Prevent AI from moving sensitive data outside approved boundaries.
Detailed Requirement: Configure DLP policies for AI-related data movement including copy, paste, upload, and sharing scenarios.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Microsoft Purview DLP / Defender for Cloud Apps
Generic Tooling Category: DLP / CASB / email and web controls
Evidence of Completion: DLP policy configuration and test evidence.
Fail Condition: Sensitive data can be moved through AI tools without DLP coverage.
Why it Matters: DLP is the enforcement layer for data handling policy.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the odds of sensitive data leaving approved boundaries through prompts, uploads, or automations.
Customer Conversation Track: What stops sensitive data from leaving approved boundaries through AI tools?

Customer Objective: Control what AI-accessible content is shared externally.
Detailed Requirement: Review and restrict external sharing for repositories and sites that AI tools access.
Evidence of Completion: Sharing review report and policy evidence.
Fail Condition: AI-accessible content is shared externally without review.
Why it Matters: External sharing combined with AI access creates compound risk.
End Customer Business Value: Finds and reduces hidden exposure that AI would otherwise surface faster and at greater scale.
Customer Conversation Track: Where is overshared content creating hidden AI exposure today?

Customer Objective: Ensure AI-generated content follows retention rules.
Detailed Requirement: Review whether AI-generated documents, logs, and outputs are covered by existing retention policies.
Evidence of Completion: Retention policy mapping for AI content types.
Fail Condition: AI-generated content accumulates without retention coverage.
Why it Matters: Retention gaps create compliance and storage risk.
End Customer Business Value: Improves both defensibility and AI quality by keeping content sources clean, governed, and reviewable.
Customer Conversation Track: Is the content feeding AI clean, governed, and defensible when reviewed later?

Customer Objective: Exclude sensitive repositories from AI access.
Detailed Requirement: Maintain a list of repositories, sites, or data sources that are explicitly excluded from AI tool access.
Evidence of Completion: Restricted repository list with exclusion evidence.
Fail Condition: All repositories are accessible to AI without explicit exclusion decisions.
Why it Matters: Some data should never be in AI scope regardless of permissions.
End Customer Business Value: Defines clear AI data boundaries so the customer can unlock value without exposing the wrong content.
Customer Conversation Track: What data should AI see, and what should never be in scope?

Customer Objective: Ensure AI knowledge bases contain trustworthy content.
Detailed Requirement: Review and qualify content sources used for AI knowledge bases, ensuring accuracy, currency, and ownership.
Evidence of Completion: Qualification checklist for each knowledge source.
Fail Condition: AI knowledge bases include unreviewed or outdated content.
Why it Matters: Knowledge quality directly affects AI output quality.
End Customer Business Value: Improves both defensibility and AI quality by keeping content sources clean, governed, and reviewable.
Customer Conversation Track: Is the content feeding AI clean, governed, and defensible when reviewed later?

Customer Objective: Apply stricter controls for regulated or high-sensitivity data.
Detailed Requirement: Design and implement access models for regulated data that AI may need to reference or process.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Purview advanced labeling / DLP / access policies
Generic Tooling Category: Sensitive data governance / DSPM / access controls
Evidence of Completion: Access model documentation and implementation evidence.
Fail Condition: Regulated data is accessible to AI under general-purpose access policies.
Why it Matters: Regulated data requires purpose-specific access controls.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the odds of sensitive data leaving approved boundaries through prompts, uploads, or automations.
Customer Conversation Track: What stops sensitive data from leaving approved boundaries through AI tools?

Customer Objective: Detect and remediate oversharing as it occurs.
Detailed Requirement: Implement continuous monitoring for overshared content in AI-accessible repositories.
Cadence: Monthly or quarterly depending on change rate
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Purview DSPM for AI / SharePoint governance signals
Evidence of Completion: Monitoring configuration and alert evidence.
Fail Condition: Oversharing is reviewed only at initial cleanup with no ongoing monitoring.
Why it Matters: Permissions drift continuously and need continuous monitoring.
End Customer Business Value: Finds and reduces hidden exposure that AI would otherwise surface faster and at greater scale.
Customer Conversation Track: Where is overshared content creating hidden AI exposure today?

Customer Objective: Limit custom agent data access to what is needed.
Detailed Requirement: Configure custom agents to access only the minimum data sources required for their function.
Cadence: Per agent / workflow and annual review
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Copilot Studio / Power Platform data source settings
Evidence of Completion: Agent data scope documentation and access evidence.
Fail Condition: Custom agents have broad data access beyond their functional need.
Why it Matters: Least-privilege applies to AI agents as much as to users.
End Customer Business Value: Reduces the odds of sensitive data leaving approved boundaries through prompts, uploads, or automations.
Customer Conversation Track: What stops sensitive data from leaving approved boundaries through AI tools?

Safeguard Title: Legal hold and investigation path for AI evidence
Customer Objective: Preserve AI-related evidence when needed.
Detailed Requirement: Document how AI interaction logs, generated content, and related data can be preserved for legal hold or investigation.
Cadence: Annual review and after relevant matters
Evidence of Completion: Legal hold procedure and test evidence.
Fail Condition: AI evidence cannot be preserved or produced when legally required.
Why it Matters: eDiscovery for AI is an emerging requirement.
End Customer Business Value: Improves both defensibility and AI quality by keeping content sources clean, governed, and reviewable.
Customer Conversation Track: Is the content feeding AI clean, governed, and defensible when reviewed later?

Customer Objective: Know who is using AI and how.
Detailed Requirement: Establish baseline adoption metrics including active users, feature usage, and engagement patterns.
Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Microsoft 365 usage reports / Viva Insights where applicable
Evidence of Completion: Adoption dashboard or report with baseline metrics.
Fail Condition: AI is deployed without visibility into actual usage patterns.
Why it Matters: You cannot manage what you cannot see.
End Customer Business Value: Gives the customer real visibility into adoption so leaders can support what is working and fix what is not.

Customer Objective: Find AI tools employees are using without approval.

Microsoft Tool Recommendation: Defender for Cloud Apps / Defender for Endpoint visibility
Evidence of Completion: Shadow AI discovery report with categorized findings.
Fail Condition: Unapproved AI usage is invisible to security and IT.
Why it Matters: Shadow AI is the largest unmanaged risk in most environments.
End Customer Business Value: Helps catch risky behavior sooner, reducing the odds that shadow AI or misuse turns into a larger event.
Customer Conversation Track: Can you detect risky or unapproved AI behavior before it becomes an incident?

Customer Objective: Ensure AI activity can be investigated when needed.

Evidence of Completion: Audit coverage confirmation and sample investigation.
Fail Condition: AI activity cannot be investigated through audit logs.
Why it Matters: Audit is the foundation for incident response and compliance.

Customer Objective: Catch AI quality problems early.

Evidence of Completion: Sampling process and initial results.

Customer Objective: Detect potentially harmful AI usage patterns.

Customer Objective: Track whether AI investment is delivering returns.
Detailed Requirement: Create a scorecard tracking AI costs, adoption, and business value metrics.
Evidence of Completion: Cost and value scorecard with trend data.
Fail Condition: AI spend continues without value measurement.

Evidence of Completion: Agent and model inventory with owner and status.
Fail Condition: AI assets proliferate without inventory or ownership.
Why it Matters: You cannot govern what you have not inventoried.

Customer Objective: Detect when AI configurations change unexpectedly.
Detailed Requirement: Review AI-related configurations periodically to detect drift from approved baselines.

Fail Condition: AI configurations drift without detection or review.
Why it Matters: Drift is a common source of security and compliance gaps.
End Customer Business Value: Keeps the AI estate governable by tracking what exists and surfacing changes before they become problems.

Customer Objective: Learn from user experience to improve AI deployment.
